title: "tristate in top-level IO" despite not declaring an input wire as tristate.
body:
i have the following design in `c.v`:
```verilog
module a (
  input wire port0
);
  wire port0_in;

  assign port0_in = port0 !== 1'bz;

endmodule

module c ();
  a inst (
    .port0()
  );
endmodule
```

When compiling `c` as top with`verilator --cc --exe ./a.cpp c.v --top c`, it reports:
```
%Error-UNSUPPORTED: c.v:12:6: Unsupported: tristate in top-level IO: 'port0'
                            : ... note: In instance 'c'
   12 |     .port0()
      |      ^~~~~
                    ... For error description see https://verilator.org/warn/UNSUPPORTED?v=5.030
%Error: Exiting due to 1 error(s)
        ... See the manual at https://verilator.org/verilator_doc.html for more assistance.

```

When compiling `a` as top, or marking `port0` as `inout`, the error goes away.

I'm wondering whether this is an expected behaviour when there is a comparison between an input port and `1'bz`. I'm using v5.030.
